exemplar.title: An Experiment to Investigate how the Duration between Learning and Recall Affects the Recall of Visually Presented Items

detailedExemplarData.title: An Experiment to Investigate how the Duration between Learning and Recall Affects the Recall of Visually Presented Items

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Aim of the investigation is clearly stated and its relevance is explained in relation to the Multi-Store Model and practical applications.
Theory description accurately details the sensory, STM and LTM stores and explicitly links the STM duration claim to the investigation.
Independent and dependent variables are precisely operationalised with clear measurement procedures, ensuring replicability.
Both directional and null hypotheses are correctly stated and operationalised, demonstrating thorough planning.
weaknesses: Theoretical description, while strong, could elaborate on sensory register pathways and encoding differences to deepen context.
Relevance explanation is clear but lacks exceptional depth or consideration of broader applications beyond the immediate scope.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments placed two elements in band 5 and one in band 6. The student meets most top-band requirements with slight room for deeper theoretical elaboration. A score of 5 reflects strong performance with minor areas for growth.

strengths: The repeated-measures design is well explained and justified by reference to controlling participant variables.
Controlled variables are not only listed but also explained with rationale, demonstrating understanding of extraneous influences.
Materials are comprehensively listed and linked to appendices, supporting procedural clarity and replicability.
weaknesses: Sampling technique description is incomplete and truncated, lacking full explanation of opportunity sampling limitations.
Justification for cultural balance and sample characteristics could be more detailed to strengthen generalisability argument.
Alternative research designs are not compared, limiting depth of design justification.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Design and variables/materials clearly meet band-4 explanations; sampling is at the lower edge of the top band. Overall meets the criteria for a top-band score of 4 in Exploration.

strengths: Appropriate descriptive (means, SDs) and inferential (one-tailed Wilcoxon) statistics are applied accurately.
Graphical presentation is correctly labelled, addresses the hypothesis, and presents data clearly.
Findings interpretation links statistical results to the hypothesis, showing solid integration of data and research question.
Choice of Wilcoxon Signed-Ranks test is justified and appropriate for the design.
weaknesses: Effect size and exact p-value are not reported, limiting completeness of inferential reporting.
Graph lacks error bars or variability indicators, reducing visual representation of data dispersion.
Sample size (N) is omitted from the graph/table captions, limiting transparency.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands sit at a strong band-5 level with minor reporting omissions. A score of 5 reflects accurate and appropriate analysis with small areas for improvement.

strengths: Modifications are explicitly linked to identified limitations (e.g., counterbalancing for practice effects) and fully justified.
Strengths and limitations are stated, described and clearly relevant to the investigationâs validity.
Discussion of findings effectively references Peterson & Peterson and the Multi-Store Model, integrating theory and results.
weaknesses: Discussion could integrate competing theories (e.g., Working Memory Model) to provide more critical depth.
Real-world educational implications are not fully explored in the conclusion.
Evaluation of sample size limitations could be more critical and detailed.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: With one strand at band 6 (modifications) and two at band 5 (findings discussion; strengths/limitations), the overall performance aligns with band 5 descriptors. The score of 5 fairly reflects strong evaluative skills with scope for broader critical reflection.

strandSelections.reasoning: A correctly labelled bar graph depicts means for both conditions and directly addresses the hypothesis.  Axes and units are clear; absence of error bars is a small flaw, so a strong 5 within top band.

Findings are not only stated but **interpreted** in light of statistical significance and explicitly linked back to the experimental hypothesis.  The interpretation is correct but brief on practical implications, hence 5 rather than 6.

Both IV and DV are **clearly identified and operationalised** (IV: 6 s vs 18 s delay timed with a stopwatch; DV: number of correctly recalled letters out of 15 within 5 s).  Research and null hypotheses are stated in directional and non-directional form respectively.  All rubric demands are fully met; hence top of band = 6.

Sampling technique (opportunity email recruitment) and participant characteristics are given, with brief justification about ignorance of psychology and cultural balance.  Explanation of **choice** is partial, so award bottom of top band = 3.

The student gives a sound description of the Multi-Store Model (encoding, capacity, duration, transfer to LTM) and **explicitly links** the STM duration claim to the present investigation.  Connection is logical and accurate; a couple of details (eg sensory register pathways) are brief, so a mark of 5ânot full 6âcaptures a strong yet slightly improvable exposition.

The student discusses results in relation to Peterson & Peterson and the MSM, offering theoretical explanation (trace decay, recency effect).  Depth is good, though discussion could integrate more competing theories; thus band top but reserve perfect score, 5.

The aim (effect of delay length on recall of consonants) is stated early and the student **explains its relevance** by linking the outcome to support for the multi-store model and to practical academic value for students.  The explanation is clear though not exceptionally detailed, meriting high-band performance but with minor depth still possible, so a mid-to-high 5 is appropriate.

Multiple strengths and limitations are **explained and clearly relevant** to the investigation (design, sample, procedure).  Reasoning shows understanding but evaluation could be more critical regarding small sample size, so 5 fits.

Several controlled variables are listed **with rationale** (constant list length, presentation time, standardised instructions).  Materials are itemised.  Because the student explains why each control is important, this strand sits solidly in the 3-4 band; fuller quantitative justification could raise to perfection, so 4 is fitting.

Each limitation is followed by a **specific, justified modification** (counterbalancing for practice effects, choosing HL maths students for arithmetic standardisation, redefining DV difficulty).  The explicit linkage and justification meet all top-band descriptors, so full 6 is warranted.

Design (repeated measures lab) is **explained**, not just described: the student justifies choice by reference to eliminating participant-variable differences in memory.  Little mention of alternative designs, but explanation suffices for the upper band score 4.

Appropriate descriptive (mean, SD) and inferential (one-tailed Wilcoxon for related scores; critical comparison, p-value) statistics are used **accurately**.  Minor reporting omissions (effect size not provided) prevent full 6, but analysis is essentially correct, so 5.

annotations.comment: Graph is correctly labeled and directly addresses the hypothesis by visually contrasting mean recall across conditions.
Include the sample size (N) in the table or caption to contextualize the statistical values and enhance transparency.
The aim is clearly stated and its relevance is explained in relation to both the Multi-Store Model and practical academic applications, fulfilling the rubricâs top band requirements.
Theory description accurately details the sensory, STM, and LTM stores and links the STM duration claim to the investigation, demonstrating strong understanding.
Consider elaborating on the sensory register pathways and encoding differences to deepen the theoretical context and strengthen the link to the investigation.
The independent variable is operationalised precisely with exact timings and measurement tools, ensuring replicability and clear testing of the delay effect.
The dependent variable is fully operationalised by specifying scoring procedure, trial sets, and time constraints, meeting the rubricâs highest standard.
Both directional and null hypotheses correctly identify and operationalise the IV and DV, demonstrating thorough planning and adherence to rubric criteria.
The null hypothesis is stated non-directionally and accurately reflects the variable relationship, completing the hypothesis requirement.
The repeated measures lab design is well justified by eliminating participant-variable differences; this moves beyond description into explanation.
To enhance, compare with alternative designs (e.g., independent groups) to fully justify your choice in context.
Participant demographics and rationale for chosen age group and psychological background are described, supporting sampling justification.
Explain how cultural balance in the sample specifically impacts generalizability to strengthen the sampling rationale.
The description of the sampling technique is incomplete and truncated; ensure full explanation of opportunity sampling procedure and its limitations.
Controlled variables are not only listed but explained with rationale, demonstrating understanding of extraneous variables.
Consider adding validation measures (e.g., pilot timing tests) to demonstrate control reliability for presentation duration.
The material list including instructions, stimuli, and forms is comprehensive and linked to appendices, showing thorough preparation.
Procedure is detailed step-by-step with measures to reduce demand characteristics, supporting replicability and validity.
Specify exact timing checks between trials and who conducted timing to further strengthen procedural clarity.
Descriptive statistics are clearly presented with means and SDs for both delay conditions, facilitating comparison.
Strengths and limitations of the repeated measures design are well-explained and relevant to practice effects, aligning with rubric descriptors.
Modification via counterbalancing is explicitly linked to the identified practice effect and fully justified, meeting top-band criteria.
Add error bars representing variability (e.g., SD or SEM) to visually indicate statistical dispersion and confidence.
The interpretation links percentage change in mean recall to the hypothesis, reinforcing data-driven conclusions.
The choice and description of the one-tailed Wilcoxon Signed Ranks test is appropriate for the repeated measures and directional hypothesis.
The main text omits the p-value and effect size; include these to fully report inferential results according to APA guidelines.
Discussion effectively relates findings to Peterson & Peterson and the Multi-Store Model, demonstrating theoretical integration.
Consider referencing alternative theories (e.g., Working Memory Model) to provide a critical discussion and show awareness of competing explanations.
Sample modification to include HL maths students addresses arithmetic ability as a confounding variable with clear justification.
When adjusting the DV to count complete sets, specify how this alters scoring protocol to ensure operational clarity and measurement consistency.
Expand the conclusion by discussing real-world educational implications and potential applications of the findings beyond theoretical support.
